preFix.title: Froe riving technique
preFix.subtitle: Splitting a green-wood billet along the grain
preFix.excerpt: How to split a green-wood billet cleanly along the grain using a froe and beetle. The starting step for any riven-blank project: spoons, mallets, chair stiles, pegs. Splitting with the grain produces stronger, more predictable blanks than sawing across it.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the froe-and-maul method. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for riving and the billet selection guidelines. The method is consistent with descriptions in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on green-woodwork tools.

content.text: Riving splits a
billet
along its grain using a
froe
and
beetle
. A
riven

blank is stronger than a sawn blank because the split follows the fibres. Sawn blanks cut across fibres at any angle the saw happens to run; riven blanks follow the grain perfectly, which makes the finished piece less likely to split in use. It is the starting step for spoon blanks, mallet handles, chair stiles, and basket stakes.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
When to use it
Riving works on
green wood

and on straight-grained seasoned logs. The tool cannot follow curved or interlocked grain; for twisted or knotty wood, a saw gives more predictable results. Good riving candidates are straight-grained ash, oak, sycamore, hazel, and sweet chestnut.
Setting up

Place the billet end-up on the chopping block or a low, heavy piece of end-grain timber. The block needs to be at a height where you can drive the beetle with a comfortable swing without crouching. A knee-height block is usually right for standing work.
Starting the split

Look at the end-grain of the billet. The growth rings radiate out from the pith (centre). For a halved split, place the froe blade across the end-grain through the centre of the pith, aligned with the long axis of the billet. Drive the beetle down onto the back of the froe blade two or three sharp blows. The blade should bite 20 mm into the end-grain.

Once the blade has started the split, lever the froe handle toward you or away from you to open the crack. The lever action continues the split down the length of the billet. If the split runs to one side (following a grain deviation), tilt the froe handle toward the thin side to steer the split back to centre.
Controlling the split

The split tends to run toward the thinner piece. To steer it straight, apply lever pressure toward the side that is running thin. This is the basic feedback loop of riving: read the split, adjust the lever. In straight-grained timber, two lever adjustments are enough for a clean 40 cm split.

For longer billets, use a pair of splitting wedges driven alternately down the crack once the froe has opened it 10 cm or more. Walk the wedges down the split, keeping both wedges in the crack at all times.
What a correct split looks like

A clean riven surface is smooth, slightly glossy, and follows the fibre lines without stepping. The fibres on both faces run parallel. An unsteered split that runs off will show a step on one face where the break crossed a ring boundary.
Common mistakes
Starting the froe off-centre on the end-grain: the split runs to one side from the start. Always place the blade through the pith.
Not responding to the split direction early enough: once the split has run to the edge, it cannot be steered back. Read the crack face and adjust the lever every 5 cm.

Using a blunt froe: a dull froe crushes the fibres rather than parting them, producing a rough split face and requiring more beetle force. Re-sharpen on a coarse India stone when the bevel no longer takes hair.
